message.content: Adversaries may upload tools to third-party or adversary-controlled infrastructure to facilitate their operations. These tools, which can be open or closed source, free or commercial, are not inherently malicious but can be repurposed for malicious activities. Commonly, adversaries stage these tools on compromised or rented infrastructure, or on web services like GitHub or Platform-as-a-Service offerings. This tactic supports operations such as Ingress Tool Transfer, where tools are made available to victim networks. Adversaries may also have compromised machines download tools directly from third-party hosting sites. Notable groups like UNC3890, Lazarus Group, and Threat Group-3390 have employed this technique, staging tools on their infrastructure or compromised websites. The potential impact includes unauthorized access and control over victim systems, enabling further malicious activities. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting outbound network traffic, employing network segmentation, and using application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized tool execution. Additionally, regular audits of web services and infrastructure for unauthorized tools can help detect and prevent exploitation.
